sentences.text: From the outside, people lived, they could see in out-- from the outside that was a camp, that people were killed over there.
They could smell it, because of the bodies they were burning.
It was time, many times, even that food what we got from them, we couldn't eat of the smell of the burning bodies.
From the lazaret.
From the lazaret.
They burned, all the garbage went into that pit, and the bodies.
I think [NON-ENGLISH] with the children were burning together.
It was a horrible smell, horrible smell, the, the flesh and the stink of it.
And sometimes, very, very sometimes, it happened many times.
We were asking, are there anything which could be remembered?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The scream of the woman from the concentration camp.
They could-- you hair could stood up, just listening to the screaming of the people, before they were gassed.
The gassing in Treblinka, it was a different one than any places.
Yes.
And not only that, the killings were done by a motor to take out the air from the room.
That people couldn't have nothing to breathe with.
And then when they put in the gas, the people were already dead.

[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] It was also many, many times [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that people from the working kommando in the camp I, something they did wrong or to them it looked wrong, we, one another, has to drag down to that lazaret.
And before the lazaret, there were over there, Ukrainian with a gun, a bullet in the head, and throwing into the lazaret.

That from our own people, people who worked, since I was there until I escaped from there, I never saw that one of them were taken into the gas chamber, beside which some people, even my brother-in-law of mine, they need people in the second camp.
And in that camp, they cut off in the morning, marching to the place where we had breakfast, cut off 20-30 people.
And they took to the second camp.
Now, many-- I want to mention that-- many, many in our camp, camp number I, in every barrack going out in the morning, we find two, three dead, committed suicide.
Because they couldn't take any longer.
They would string, with a shirt. spme of them, they had tablets.
Yes.
There were quite a number of doctors, which they had tablets with them.
I would like to mention also some of my friends, very brave of them, they were chosen to stay to work together with us.
One of them was a close friend of mine who was also a barber.
Let me get that.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I get you the name in a-- in a few minutes.
They choose him that he should stay and work, him and another one.
That's separate, not all-- not both together.
They refused it.
And they run into the gas chamber.
One of them was Shlomo Dorfman.
And the other was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] gee, I don't remember the name of them, the--
No.
To the gas chamber.
When they-- when they took men in, they took off their clothes, and they run in with the men to the gas chamber, two of them.
Yes.
No.
No.
They were chosen to work at the camp number I by the selecting of the clothes and other things, just like myself and anybody else.
But instead, when they saw the wife's death, and family went into the gas chamber, they couldn't resist, and they went into the same thing.
And that happened twice, only with those two, which I know.
It happened to my brother-in-law, which also he killed himself.
Because his wife and the whole family went in.
His name was Jacobowitz, Shimshon.
Very highly intelligent, very intelligent man.
Oh, it happened also a lot of people got killed, if you want to know.
Over there, you couldn't do-- let's say if you want to go to the toilet.
You couldn't go.
They have-- you have to ask to let you, and to a time of it.
One young kid by the name of Schlifka, he begged.
He could not helped himself-- help himself.
He had to go.
An SS man took out a gun and killed him.
You want to know more stories.
I don't want you to listen to more stories of that, horrible stories.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Going back, I will stop, but I'm going back to the escape, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] how I escaped.
We went in to that barrack at night, around 9 o'clock in the winter.
It was cold, dark, on our bellies.
We went out from that barrack.
And we knew that one way to escape is through that lazaret where people and garbage were burning.
And we had to cross that.
Otherwise, if we have to make-- we have to make six, seven gates [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with barbed wires.
But over there, there was only one, through that lazaret.
So we went through.
Myself, a cousin of mine by the name of Haskel Kaufman, and a friend of mine by the name of Chil Berkowitz.
We went through.
Yes.
Yes.
We organized.
We had to do this.
We organized it, we had to.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we took some clothes on top of us that the fire shouldn't catch us, and went through those burning people with the garbage to that one gate.
You could do it.
If your life is in danger, you do anything without even counting what you're doing.
It wasn't locked.
It was the Ukrainian way.
But it happened.
We got a glimpse of them.
There were two of them.
They're all the way drunk, or they were sleeping.
And we did it.
We got to the gate, crawled over.
Also on the barbed wire, we put clothes on top of it that we shouldn't touch [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the barbed wires.
We went over.
Went over, it was around I would say, it took us more than a hour, about 10 o'clock.
From 10 o'clock at night we start running far away from Treblinka, but running.
At 5 o'clock in the morning, we were almost in the same place, running around until we went in to the camp of the Ukrainian.
We were at Russian, then we saw the stars.
And we start running a different way.
Just about-- I, I would say, not even a half a mile from Treblinka, by running five, seven hours.
All around, we didn't know exactly where to go.
I would say I was.
Yeah.
I would say, yeah that I was.
Yes.
Even the cousin of mine, he was older than me, 10 years older.
Yes.
No.
Ammunition, absolutely not.
Money, yes, we had.
We had money with us.
We-- the people that came to Treblinka, they left their bundles.
By looking through the clothes, we took out whatever it was there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Most of it, we have to put in, in the valise.
But larger bill, and some other things, we put in, in our pockets, which it could not be in a pocket, because we were searched.
We took from this place.
We put in another place.
Well, it happened that we had money.
And all the people that escaped, they had to have money to buy themselves out from the Polacks.
Underneath, from here up to here.
We had a band, like a bandage.
And we had around the money there.
We came to a place where it was the big area by the name of [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From about 5 o'clock in the morning until it got a little bit light, we stayed in the forest near the-- near the water.
From there, we saw a woman coming out.
When she came out-- and she knew exactly, because altogether it was about three miles from the camp of Treblinka.
After about five months, little after five months.
That was in January 1943.
A jacket.
We are not heavy dressed.
Because with heavy dress, you couldn't walk, you couldn't run.
No.
No.
It wasn't-- at that time it wasn't snowing.
We went over to her.
We can't go in, she said.
No Germans who are-- excuse me.
The Germans are all around, better escape from there.
All of a sudden, we saw somebody going with a horse and wagon.
Went over to him.
I said, can we sit over the day at your place.
He said my place, you cannot do.
Because I live in that little village, in the middle of the village.
But there is a place right in front of the village.
Go over there.
We went over there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And over there was like, like a little village.
The head of the little village, I would call him here, like little mayor, not even a mayor, something like that.
That was the head from the village.
He saw us.
He came up-- came over.
He said in Polish, here, go up to this and this place, you know, where cows, other things were.
And we were over there.
He brought us food, hot food, which is something special, in, in, in so many months.
And we was there over the day.
At night he said to us, you wouldn't know where to go, how.
Because we wanted to go to the railroad station, to buy tickets, and to go back to the city of Czestochowa.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
So he took us, a place where we would have to go at least a whole day.
It took us two hours, and he came with us.
We went then to another village.
He left us over there.
In the morning, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we saw another woman coming out of a place.
So I went over there.
We came into that woman.
She took us [INAUDIBLE].
And it was those two men with me together.
She make the place also outside, outside the house.
There was like, I would say, you keep over there animals, other things.
We're freezing over there.
We had no food.
Every day cost us a fortune of money.
Because they were smart Polacks.
They took any money they could get off us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Until one day they find out, as one of these friends, Berkowitz, he was a tailor, excellent tailor.
So they needed clothes, and a warm jacket.
We did give them the money.
They went over to fur store, boot stuff.
He make it for them.
It was something beautiful.
And this guy went out.
Everybody went over to him.
Where did you buy this, this?
So he had a cousin over there.
He told him the real story, that he has three Jews hidden, and one of them is a tailor.
And he make that for him.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And in his place, at daytime we were in that outside house.
It wasn't a house.
At night he took us to his house.
He had a big room divided about like this here.
How would you call that, from clothes, a divide?
A partition?
Like a partition.
On the other side, that was a nice girl from Warsaw.
You know, the boys were there.
They were sitting in the place behind that curtain.
We couldn't breathe, we couldn't do nothing.
Until this man came over, this cousin.
He said, I know everything about you.
I got a sewing machine.
Come over to me.
You make me one.
And we came over to them.
According to where he lived, and the guy we were before, he was-- he was living in a villa.
We came in over there.
The woman at the desk about money, let's say for 500 zloty, which according to our money was about $5.
She maked a meal like for a king.
Where in the other place, we paid 10 times as much, all we had is potatoes, cooked to be a roll that we shouldn't be able to eat much.
We were over there about two weeks at that place.
And it was really nice, the people were very nice.
And according my heart, I suspected that she must be Jewish.
They had two kids, a son 9-year-old, a daughter 7 years old.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we left, she said, please.
Somebody if you survive, let me know what happened to you.
At night, we left the place over there.
Two of them, her husband and her husband's brother, went with us to the railroad station.
On the way to the rail station, all of sudden behind us, two Germans with guns.
And we were shocked.
I really didn't know what to say.
But my cousin, [INAUDIBLE] me and I think nothing to say.
But we find out that people from that place they had no documents, because they changed the documents to other one.
When they got us document, well, we have no documents.
Because they took away the documents.
We're going to get new.
Where are you going to go today?
And they looked at us.
OK, let's just go.
We got to the railroad.
We were waiting outside, and they went in to buy tickets for us.
They bought tickets.
Those two men.
The man who took us into his house and his brother.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We went into the railroad.
Then the train came.
We went from there to Warsaw, which is about-- I would say about 80 miles.
Not at all.
No.
Nobody did.
Went with people, a lot of people was in the train.
We got there to Warsaw at the main railroad station.
Now as you know, not, not Jews, or even Polacks were not allowed after 8 o'clock to run around.
But here, you got two Jewish men running around.
Nobody came over to ask us.
But we needed tickets to go another 240 kilometer from Warsaw.
So I went over to the man who sold the tickets.
He said, he cannot give us because the farthest we can go is only 25 kilometers.
We needed to 225. so I took out, at that time, money.
I don't know how much money.
I put on the table.
So we had already papers from the Gestapo, papers from the [NON-ENGLISH], which means from the city, and from the railroad that we're going.
We got into Czestochowa.
So that's where your destination was, Czestochowa?
Yes.
Yeah, we go back.
Because I myself, I thought my brother's alive, or somebody from the family, and other people, too.
We got there.
Oh, we got to Czestochowa.
It was also at daytime.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We waited in a place.
I saw some friends of mine, Polacks.
They recognized me.
But they didn't come over.
They didn't, not at all.
Like they never saw me, I never saw them.
And at night, we went over to the ghetto.
There was already a small ghetto.
Very, very much.
It was only three small streets.
I would say in all the streets were maybe 20 houses, not even 20 houses for all the people.
And at that time it was about 5,000 people.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we went over to a policeman, Polish policeman.
We want to go into the ghetto.
Usually people would escape from the ghetto.
And we wanted to go back to the ghetto.
So we give him-- I don't remember exactly how much money.
We went in over there.
We got over there.
They didn't know what to say.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
Because when we tried to tell the people, like [INAUDIBLE], oh, you were away with us, and my folks, my, my, my mother, or father, or wife.
So we told them.
Nobody's alive.
They all got killed.
Yeah.
Well, you want to make a panic, in the little ghetto?
But that's the truth.
Nobody, but nobody could believe us.
And nobody did believe that something like that had happened.
What you want to say?
You want to tell me they killed my wife for what?
Go tell the man how his wife perished.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It happened.
So far we went to that some of the Jewish informants informed the head man from the Gestapo the men which he make the selection of who should be alive or who should be dead, by the name of [?
Dagenhart, ?] that some people arrived here from Treblinka.
They were there, and they're telling us horrible stories.
So he looked at them.
He said like that, if they could escape from over there, let them be, just like that.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that how it happened that I was inside.
But even inside we have also a close organization.
We try to make an uprising.
Inside the small ghetto now.
Inside the small ghetto.
Inside the small ghetto, we have some-- a group specially, between five and six people from Treblinka.
That had escaped from Treblinka.
In Czestochowa, yeah.
I met them over there.
These other people was a man.
I mentioned before, by the name Eisner, Jacob Eisner.
There was Moishe Rappaport, Aaron Gilbert, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and our three, that is six.
There was more there.
But I don't remember their names.
Yes, yes.
The two brothers, Harmonik.
Yes.
Yes.
We tried to do that.
But it is-- it was very-- even between Jewish people, it was very hard.
Because-- because it was a lot informers.
